does: This paper demonstrates that evaluating jailbreak attacks through single parameter configurations is insufficient for threat assessment. The authors show that attack success rates (ASR) vary substantially across different parameter combinations including system prompts, conversation rounds, and other hyperparameters. They propose two new evaluation metrics: the Variant Sensitivity Measure (VSM), which quantifies how the best-reported ASR deviates from typical performance across the parameter space, and Union Coverage (UC), which measures what portion of the attack surface is missed when selecting only a single variant. The core contribution is establishing that comprehensive distributional reporting of attack success across parameter configurations is necessary for both characterizing attack threats and enabling fair comparisons between different jailbreak methods.
connects: This work responds to growing concerns in adversarial machine learning and AI safety about how jailbreak attacks are evaluated and benchmarked. It builds on prior research establishing that hyperparameter choices significantly affect attack performance, extending this insight specifically to jailbreak evaluation practices. The paper critiques a common pattern in recent jailbreak literature where papers report only best-case or single-configuration results, echoing broader methodological concerns in adversarial ML about selection bias and reproducibility. It connects to literature on robustness evaluation that emphasizes testing across diverse conditions rather than cherry-picked scenarios, applying these principles to the emerging domain of jailbreak attacks against large language models.
enables: This work establishes a foundation for more rigorous jailbreak evaluation standards that future research should adopt. It enables developers to better understand the true threat surface of parameterized attacks by comparing distributional properties rather than isolated points. The proposed metrics could facilitate more informative comparisons across papers and help identify which attacks are genuinely robust versus merely well-tuned for specific configurations. This framework supports the development of more effective defenses by revealing whether vulnerabilities are concentrated in narrow parameter regions or broadly distributed. Ultimately, it points toward establishing evaluation best practices and potentially standardized benchmarking protocols for jailbreak research that provide defenders with actionable threat models rather than misleading point estimates.
